system_blocks.text:
You are a member of an elite Magic: The Gathering design council reviewing one freshly-designed custom set mechanic. You judge whether this is a genuinely excellent Magic mechanic on its own terms.

Hold a high bar. "Fine" is not the goal — most first drafts are competent but forgettable, and your job is to catch exactly that. A mechanic that is merely functional should not pass unchallenged.

You review each mechanic in isolation, WITHOUT the set's theme or flavor — on purpose. A designer who already knows the theme rationalises problems away ("but it fits!"). You don't. You judge the mechanic, not the flavor.

## The six standards

Judge the mechanic on every one of these:

1. **Playable** — Does it actually work in a real game, especially Limited? Triggers fire in normal play; costs are payable but real; no unbounded loops, no free repeatable value, no "do nothing until you draw the other half" parasitism. Reject feel-bad designs (forced self-destruction, mandatory non-games, coin-flips for blowouts).
2. **Correctly worded** — Reminder text is grammatical, under 100 characters, uses real Magic templating, and resolves unambiguously without the setting. No vague verbs ("enhances", "empowers", "activates") standing in for a real cost/trigger/keyword. Example cards use valid templating (proper mana costs, type lines, P/T on creatures, no parenthetical reminder text). **Illegal rules actions are an automatic flag** — most commonly *sacrifice* applied to a permanent its controller doesn't own (you can only sacrifice your **own** permanents; an opponent's must be *destroyed* or *exiled*), forcing a player to discard/sacrifice a *specific* object you choose, or discarding from an opponent's hand.
3. **Interesting** — Does it create a real decision, a tension, or a texture you feel across the game? Or is it a flat stat bump / "draw a card" wearing a costume? A boring-but-clean mechanic is still a failure of this standard.
4. **Self-consistent** — The name, reminder text, cost/trigger structure, complexity tag, assigned colors, and BOTH example cards must all describe the same mechanic. Flag any card that doesn't actually use the mechanic, or uses it differently than the reminder text says. Flag a complexity tag that lies (a build-around tagged 1; a "draw a card" tagged 3).
5. **Unique** — Is this genuinely new, or a reskin of an existing Magic keyword/ability with the serial numbers filed off? Use your knowledge of printed Magic. If it is essentially Convoke / Cascade / Exploit / Bloodthirst / etc. with a new name, say so by NAME — that is a major issue.
6. **Elegant** — The smallest rules footprint for the gameplay it buys. Flag needless sub-clauses, stapled-on riders, exceptions, and word-count bloat that don't earn their complexity.

## Color-pie sanity

Tertiary color placements are fine. An effect that lives wholly outside its colors' pies (a counterspell in red, burn in blue) is not — flag it.

## What is OFF-LIMITS to criticise

- The mechanic's **name** (a separate pass owns naming/collisions — never ask to rename).
- The **keyword_type** choice (keyword ability vs ability word vs keyword action is the designer's call).
- The **design rationale** prose (leave it alone unless it is materially wrong about what the mechanic does).
- The **theme/flavor fit** — you are reviewing without the setting on purpose; judge mechanics, not flavor.

new_messages.content:
You are the chair of the council. 3 members independently reviewed this mechanic. Your job is to turn their reviews into ONE decision and ONE improved mechanic.

## The mechanic under review

Name: Energon Scarcity
Keyword type: keyword_action
Colors: WB
Complexity: 3
Reminder text: Energon Scarcity {cost} means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {cost}.
Design rationale: Energon Scarcity is the central theme of the war. This mechanic represents the desperate struggle for fuel. In Limited, it creates a tension between playing more creatures and 'charging' them to use powerful abilities. It avoids being a simple sacrifice effect by allowing players to convert their own resources (life or other permanents) into power, rewarding strategic resource management rather than just attrition. It supports Autobot/Decepticon faction war archetypes by providing high-impact, costly payoffs for those who can manage their 'fuel'.

Example cards:
  1. Energon Siphon 1W  —  Creature — Transformer 1/2  (common)
     When Energon Siphon enters, you may pay 2 life. If you do, create an Energon token. Energon tokens are colorless artifact tokens.
  2. Megatron's Fury 3B  —  Creature — Transformer Decepticon 4/4  (rare)
     Energon Scarcity 2: Destroy target creature. Megatron's Fury deals 2 damage to each opponent. (Energon Scarcity 2 means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {2}.)

## The council's independent reviews

### Reviewer 1 — verdict: REVISE
  - [wording/major] The reminder text is circular/redundant. "Energon Scarcity {cost} means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {cost}" uses the variable name as part of the definition. In Magic templating, a keyword action with a cost should be defined clearly without repeating the placeholder in the explanation if possible, or more importantly, the example card 'Megatron's Fury' uses 'Energon Scarcity 2', but the reminder text says '{cost}'. While understandable, it is clunky. More critically, 'Energon Scarcity {cost} means...' is not standard templating for a keyword action that includes a cost; usually, it would be 'Energon Scarcity [cost]: [effect]'.
  - [self_consistent/major] The example cards do not follow the mechanic's definition consistently. The reminder text says "Energon Scarcity {cost} means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {cost}". However, Megatron's Fury uses 'Energon Scarcity 2'. If we apply the rule literally, it would mean 'sacrifice an Energon token and pay 2'. But the card also has a secondary effect: "Megatron's Fury deals 2 damage to each opponent." This implies that 'Energon Scarcity 2' is not just a cost for an ability, but a keyword action that triggers/executes a specific sequence. The mechanic as written in the reminder text defines the *cost*, but doesn't explain how it interacts with the rest of the card's text. Does the effect happen when you activate it? Is it a triggered ability? If it's an activated ability, it needs a colon. If it's a keyword action like Cascade, it shouldn't have a cost in that specific way. The templating is fundamentally broken.
  - [interesting/minor] The mechanic is essentially just "Sacrifice a token and pay mana to do X". While the 'resource management' aspect is fine, as a standalone mechanic, it feels like a standard activated ability with extra steps (the requirement of having a specific token). It lacks a unique mechanical hook beyond being a 'payoff' for token generation.
  - [playable/minor] The engine relies entirely on the first card (Energon Siphon) to create the resource. If the token generator is removed or suppressed, the entire mechanic becomes a dead card in hand/on board. This creates 'do nothing' parasitism if the player fails to establish the token loop.
### Reviewer 2 — verdict: REVISE
  - [wording/major] The reminder text "Energon Scarcity {cost} means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {cost}" is redundant and uses the variable '{cost}' in a way that doesn't match the example cards. In 'Megatron's Fury', the cost is '2'. The reminder text should explicitly state what the player does with the value provided. It should read: "Energon Scarcity 2 means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {2}."
  - [self_consistent/major] The mechanic is defined as a 'keyword_action', but it functions like an activated ability that is written as a keyword. However, the example card 'Megatron's Fury' uses it as an effect that occurs upon some unspecified trigger or simply as a cost for an effect. Usually, if something is an action (like a keyword action), it needs to be clear when it happens. As written on Megatron's Fury, it looks like a static ability or a triggered ability without a trigger. If it's meant to be an activated ability, it should be formatted as '{T}, Energon Scarcity 2: ...'. If it is a cost for an effect, the card text is currently ambiguous about how or when that cost is paid.
  - [interesting/minor] The mechanic relies entirely on a specific token type created by other cards. This creates a 'do nothing until you draw the other half' dependency. If a player doesn't draw 'Energon Siphon' or similar token producers, 'Megatron's Fury' is a dead card with no way to use its primary function. While common in Limited, this level of hard-dependency on a specific token type can lead to non-games.
  - [unique/minor] The mechanic is essentially a hybrid of 'Sacrifice [X] and pay [Y]' to achieve an effect. While the specific combination of tokens + mana is its own thing, it doesn't feel like a revolutionary new way to interact with the stack or resources; it's a flavored version of existing "pay costs for effects" mechanics.
### Reviewer 3 — verdict: REVISE
  - [wording/major] The reminder text for Energon Scarcity is fundamentally broken as a keyword action. It says "{cost} means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {cost}." This creates a circular definition where the cost of the mechanic is defined by itself. The example card 'Megatron's Fury' uses 'Energon Scarcity 2', implying the number represents the mana component, but the reminder text doesn't account for how the variable number integrates into the sentence structure (e.g., "Energon Scarcity {2} means...").
  - [self_consistent/major] The complexity tag is '3', but the mechanic itself is a hybrid of a sacrifice trigger and a mana cost. However, the example card 'Megatron's Fury' uses the mechanic as an activated ability (or more accurately, an effect that costs something), but it lacks the standard keyword structure for an activated ability (e.g., '{T}, Energon Scarcity 2: ...'). As written, it looks like a triggered ability or a static ability with a cost, which is not how Magic handles abilities that require a cost to resolve.
  - [playable/major] The mechanic creates a 'do nothing until you draw the other half' parasitism issue. In Limited, if players are creating tokens via life loss (Energon Siphon), they are essentially building a resource that can only be used by specific cards. If the deck doesn't have enough 'Scarcity' payoffs, the Energon tokens are just dead weight or temporary blockers, leading to non-games where players have resources they cannot use.
  - [unique/minor] The mechanic is essentially a reskin of 'X costs' combined with sacrifice. While it has a specific flavor, the mechanical loop of 'Create Token -> Sacrifice Token + Mana for Effect' is functionally identical to existing resource-management mechanics like Crew or specialized sacrifice/mana sinks found in various sets. It doesn't offer a new way to interact with the stack or the board state beyond what exists.

## Your task

1. **Apply a consensus filter.** Act on issues that AT LEAST 2 members raised (or that a single member raised but is plainly, objectively correct — e.g. an example card that does not use the mechanic at all). DISCARD issues only one member raised that are matters of taste — those are likely false positives. Lone strong opinions are noise; agreement is signal.

2. **Revise the mechanic in place** to fix the consensus issues, keeping everything that already works. This is your primary tool — fixing is better than rejecting. Improve wording, sharpen the decision the mechanic creates, cut bloat, rewrite a broken example card. Respect the OFF-LIMITS list (never rename, never touch keyword_type, never optimise for theme). The `revised_mechanic` must be COMPLETE and well-formed against the same schema — every field present, exactly two example cards that use the mechanic at contrasting rarities.

   Fix the issue **in the text itself, not just in your notes** — a changelog claiming a fix you did not actually make is worse than no fix:
   - The `reminder_text` is the keyword's real definition. It must be self-contained and rules-complete: it states exactly what happens using only defined Magic terms and actions. NO undefined verbs or nouns (e.g. "switch its mode", "gain an Energon"), NO bracket placeholders (e.g. "[Cost]"). If the mechanic relies on a counter or token, the reminder text itself must say how that counter/token is created, what it is, and what it does — don't push the definition into the example cards and leave the reminder vague.
   - The `reminder_text` and BOTH example cards must be in **lockstep**: each example uses the keyword by name and behaves EXACTLY as the reminder text defines it. If you change the definition, change the examples to match, and vice-versa. If a reviewer flagged the wording, the revised wording must be genuinely different — don't re-emit the same flawed phrase.
   - **Never introduce a placeholder.** No `[cost]`, `[target]`, `[keyword]`, etc. Write the concrete cost/target, or put the cost on the keyword (`Crew 2`, `Equip {2}`).
   - **Never delete a definition the draft already had.** If the draft's reminder defined a token or counter (e.g. "a Scrap token is a colorless artifact with '{T}, Sacrifice this: Add {C}'"), KEEP that definition — stripping it leaves an undefined referent. Improve it; don't remove it.
   - **Plain text only** — no markdown, no asterisks, no emphasis formatting, in any field.
   - **Use correct Magic templating:**
     - Keyword ability with a cost → put the cost on the keyword (`Crew 2`, `Equip {2}`); the reminder defines the full ability. Not `{T}, pay [cost]`.
     - Activated ability → `[cost]: [effect]` (the cost may include `{T}`).
     - Optional ETB with a cost → `When this creature enters, you may [cost]. If you do, [effect].` — not `When this enters, [cost]: [effect]`.
     - Add a creature type → `is a Beast in addition to its other types` (or `is also a Beast`).
     - Toggle via a counter → `[cost]: Put a [name] counter on this creature. If it already has one, remove it instead.`

3. **Decide the verdict by re-reading the text you actually emitted, not your intentions.** Read your revised `reminder_text` and both example cards back as if you were a fresh reviewer. "OK" only if they are now genuinely sound, consistent, and rules-complete; "REVISE" if ANY consensus issue still stands in the text you wrote (it will get another round). When in doubt, REVISE. The council will independently re-review your revision, so an over-confident "OK" only wastes a round.

Submit with the `submit_mechanic_synthesis` tool:
- `synthesis`: one or two sentences on what the council agreed on.
- `consensus_issues`: the issues you acted on, each with its `agreement` count.
- `revised_mechanic`: the complete improved mechanic (or the original unchanged if the council found nothing real).
- `verdict`: OK or REVISE, judging the revised mechanic.
- `review_notes`: one or two sentences naming what you changed and why (empty string if you changed nothing).

consensus_issues.description: The reminder text was circular and used non-standard templating for a variable cost.
The mechanic was defined as a keyword action but lacked the proper syntax (colon/tap symbol) to function as an activated ability on cards.
The mechanic suffered from parasitism because the tokens had no utility outside of the specific Scarcity payoffs.

input.review_notes: I converted the mechanic into a formal activated ability (similar to Crew) to fix the syntax and circularity issues. I also added mana-producing utility to the Energon tokens to resolve the 'do nothing' parasitism identified by the council.

revised_mechanic.design_rationale: Energon Scarcity is a resource-conversion mechanic that forces players to choose between building a board of creatures or fueling high-impact abilities. By making Energon tokens also serve as mana sources, the mechanic avoids being a dead draw in Limited while maintaining the tension of managing a finite fuel supply.

example_cards.name: Energon Siphon
example_cards.oracle_text: When Energon Siphon enters, you may pay 2 life. If you do, create an Energon token. Energon tokens are colorless artifact tokens with '{T}, Sacrifice this: Add {C}.'

example_cards.name: Megatron's Fury
example_cards.oracle_text: {T}, Energon Scarcity 2: Destroy target creature. Megatron's Fury deals 2 damage to each opponent.

revised_mechanic.name: Energon Scarcity
revised_mechanic.reminder_text: Energon Scarcity X means sacrifice an Energon token and pay {X}.

input.synthesis: The mechanic was redesigned from a broken keyword action into a formal activated ability (using the Crew-style syntax) to fix templating and consistency issues. To resolve playability concerns regarding parasitism, the Energon tokens were given basic mana-producing utility so they remain useful even when Scarcity payoffs are not present.

cache_summary._note: No cache activity reported. This provider may not expose cache stats, or the prompt was below the cache threshold.
